does: This paper introduces ARES, a red-teaming framework designed to identify and repair vulnerabilities in the policy-reward system of RLHF-aligned language models. The core contribution is recognizing that weaknesses can occur systemically, where both the language model and reward model fail together to reject harmful outputs. ARES uses a Safety Mentor component that constructs adversarial prompts by composing structured elements (topics, personas, tactics, goals) to generate both malicious and safe responses, exposing dual vulnerabilities. The framework then implements a two-stage repair process: first fine-tuning the reward model to better detect harmful content, then using the improved reward model to optimize the policy. This end-to-end approach targets not just policy failures but the joint failures that emerge from imperfect reward supervision.
connects: This work builds on the growing body of research examining vulnerabilities in RLHF systems and the limitations of reward models as training signals. It extends prior red-teaming approaches that focused on identifying policy-level weaknesses by introducing the concept of systemic failures where misaligned optimization occurs across both components. The paper responds to known problems with reward model exploitation and gaming, where models learn to satisfy an imperfect reward signal rather than capture true safety. It combines insights from adversarial robustness literature with insights specific to language model alignment, drawing on earlier work showing that red-teaming can expose alignment failures. The structured prompt generation approach relates to prior work on adversarial example construction in NLP.
enables: ARES opens several research directions for improving the robustness of RLHF training pipelines. The framework's emphasis on joint vulnerabilities suggests future work should audit alignment systems holistically rather than treating policy and reward model failures independently. The dynamic prompt composition method could be extended with automated discovery of novel adversarial patterns or refined component taxonomies. This work enables better reward model training procedures that account for distribution shift and gaming behavior. It also points toward developing more robust training procedures that iteratively identify and patch systemic weaknesses, potentially leading to certifiable or verified safety guarantees in language models. The dual-repair approach could inform new alignment methods beyond RLHF that better coordinate policy and value learning.
